nativeRange.habitat: Lower Amazon River basin tributaries, oxbow pools, and marginal lakes. Generally found in clear, transparent water with low flow, soft substrates, abundant leaf litter, and aquatic vegetation.

decorPreferences: dried catappa/Indian almond leaves and leaf litter
live plants and floating cover for diffused light
flat stones or broad leaves as potential spawning sites

diet.notes: Wild diet has been documented to include algae, detritus, insects, small worms, and microcrustaceans foraged from the substrate. In the aquarium, generally accepts a wide range of small frozen and live foods (bloodworm, daphnia, baby brine shrimp, cyclops) and tends to take quality dried flakes and micro-pellets once settled. A varied diet is commonly considered to support coloration and breeding condition.

avoidWith: boisterous fin-nippers (e.g., tiger barbs, larger Puntius)
very small dwarf shrimp (predation risk, especially with hungry parents)
other territorial dwarf cichlids in small tanks (Apistogramma overlap may trigger conflict)
fast, aggressive feeders that outcompete a slow grazer at the substrate

summary: The Dwarf Flag Cichlid (Laetacara curviceps) is a peaceful South American dwarf cichlid from the lower Amazon tributaries, known for forming monogamous biparental pairs. It is generally considered one of the more approachable dwarf cichlids to keep.
careNotes:
The Dwarf Flag Cichlid is a small, peaceful cichlid from the lower Amazon basin and is commonly recommended as a more forgiving entry-point dwarf cichlid than most Apistogramma species. Adults are generally reported at around 5–8 cm total length, with females typically running slightly larger than males in the wild.

The species is best kept as a single monogamous pair in tanks of roughly 80 L (75 cm length) or larger, with quieter, mid-water community fish to draw it out. It is generally considered peaceful and is sometimes described as 'smiling' because of its upturned mouth markings. Outside of spawning it tends to share tank space without much conflict; during spawning, the pair will defend a small territory around the spawning site but is rarely problematic toward unrelated tankmates in adequately sized tanks.

A soft sandy substrate with driftwood, roots, leaf litter, and live or floating plants approximates its native habitat well. Lighting diffused by floating plants is generally recommended; strong, unbroken overhead light may cause the fish to stay hidden. Gentle filtration with low flow matches the still, sediment-poor backwaters where this species is found in the wild.

The species tolerates a fairly wide chemistry envelope (22–27°C, pH 5.5–7.5), but stability and low nitrate accumulation matter more than hitting any specific target. Regular partial water changes are commonly advised, particularly when conditioning a pair for breeding.

Unlike Apistogramma, this species does not form harems — pairs are monogamous and biparental, so it is generally acquired as a known pair or as a group of 4–6 juveniles grown out together to allow a pair to form naturally.
breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner. Forms a monogamous pair bond and typically spawns on a flat, cleaned surface (broad leaf, flat stone, or piece of driftwood) rather than inside a cave. Both parents have been reported to guard and tend eggs and fry — fanning the eggs and herding free-swimming fry as a coordinated pair, in contrast to the harem/female-only broodcare of Apistogramma. Spawning is generally considered moderately achievable in captivity; soft, slightly acidic water and a quiet tank with established cover are commonly cited triggers. Pairs are most reliably obtained by grow-out of 4–6 juveniles and removal of excess fish once a bonded pair becomes obvious.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the originally assigned Aquarium Co-Op product page (https://www.aquariumcoop.com/products/dwarf-flag-cichlid) returned HTTP 404. SeriouslyFish provided the most detailed per-species husbandry profile: scientific name, taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes/Cichliformes), 4.5–5 cm SL adult size, temperature 20–28 °C, pH 4.5–7.5, hardness 18–268 ppm, biparental substrate-spawner breeding mode, native range (lower Amazon), and habitat (clear low-flow water with leaf litter).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Cichlasomatinae), size (4.6 cm SL male / 6.6 cm female, TL up to 10 cm), temperature 22–26 °C, pH 5.2–7.5, dH 5–12, distribution (Amazon basin tributaries in Brazil). Notes minimum aquarium size of 60 cm and pair-based housing.

additional.name: Steenfott Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (20 gal / ~76 L minimum, 30+ gal ideal), temperature 75–82 °F (24–28 °C), pH 6.0–7.5, adult size 2.5–3 in (~6.4–7.6 cm), peaceful temperament with slight territoriality during breeding, monogamous biparental substrate spawning on flat surfaces, sand-and-wood decor preference, and 'one of the easier dwarf cichlids' care framing.

fish.breedingNotes: Open/substrate spawner — pair selects and cleans a flat surface (leaf, stone, or wood) and both parents guard eggs and fry. More forgiving than most Apistogramma to breed because pair behavior is monogamous and biparental rather than haremic. Soft, slightly acidic water and a quiet tank generally aid spawning.
